papers.title: Double bow shocks around young, runaway red supergiants: application to Betelgeuse
papers.author_names: Jonathan Mackey Shazrene Mohamed Hilding R. Neilson Norbert Langer Dominique M.-A. Meyer

abstract.text: A significant fraction of massive stars are moving supersonically through the interstellar medium (ISM), either due to disruption of a binary system or ejection from their parent star cluster. The interaction of their wind with the ISM produces a bow shock. In late evolutionary stages these stars may undergo rapid transitions from red to blue and vice versa on the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram, with accompanying rapid changes to their stellar winds and bow shocks. Recent 3D simulations of the bow shock produced by the nearby runaway red supergiant (RSG) Betelgeuse, under the assumption of a constant wind, indicate that the bow shock is very young (<30000 years old), hence Betelgeuse may have only recently become a RSG. To test this possibility, we have calculated stellar evolution models for single stars which match the observed properties of Betelgeuse in the RSG phase. The resulting evolving stellar wind is incorporated into 2D hydrodynamic simulations in which we model a runaway blue supergiant (BSG) as it undergoes the transition to a RSG near the end of its life. We find that the collapsing BSG wind bubble induces a bow shock-shaped inner shell around the RSG wind that resembles Betelgeuse's bow shock, and has a similar mass. Surrounding this is the larger-scale retreating bow shock generated by the now defunct BSG wind's interaction with the ISM. We suggest that this outer shell could explain the bar feature located (at least in projection) just in front of Betelgeuse's bow shock.

source_fields.sections_text: 3. Results: The simulation shows that during the evolution of a runaway red supergiant, a thin, dense shell forms around the RSG wind with mass and size consistent with AKARI observations, driven by reverse shock compression and cooling-dominated shell dynamics, while Kelvin-Helmholtz instabilities and uncertain heating/cooling balance affect shock structure and stability. 5. Conclusions: This study presents the first quantitative model showing that a massive runaway star transitioning from blue to red supergiant phase develops a transient inner shocked-shell that mimics a bow shock and closely matches observed features of Betelgeuse’s bow shock, with implications for the detection of such structures in runaway red supergiants. 1. Introduction: This paper presents the first detailed investigation of bow shock evolution during a stellar transition from blue to red via 2D hydrodynamic simulations, testing the hypothesis that Betelgeuse's observed bar feature is a remnant of its former blue supergiant or main sequence wind. 2. Description of code and methods: The paper proposes a 2D axisymmetric hydrodynamics simulation of a 15 M⊙ solar-metallicity red supergiant (RSG) star evolving from the blue loop to the RSG phase, using a stellar evolution model to drive wind properties and solving the compressible hydrodynamics equations with a freely expanding wind boundary condition to study the interaction between the stellar wind and the interstellar medium. 4. Discussion and comparison to Betelgeuse: The simulation shows that the collapsing reverse shock of a young blue supergiant (BSG) interacting with an expanding red supergiant (RSG) wind produces a bow shock with a mass of $M_s \\leq 0.002\\,M_\\odot$, consistent with observed values and explaining the low mass of Betelgeuse’s bow shock, while also reproducing the layered structure and bar-like feature attributed to the BSG contact discontinuity.

To test this possibility, we have calculated stellar evolution models for single stars which match the observed properties of Betelgeuse in the RSG phase.

The resulting evolving stellar wind is incorporated into 2D hydrodynamic simulations in which we model a runaway blue supergiant (BSG) as it undergoes the transition to a RSG near the end of its life.
